stories.headline: Update: Swalwell Resigns from Congress as New Accuser Alleges Drugging and Rape; LA Sheriff Opens Investigation
stories.summary:
Rep. Eric Swalwell formally resigned his congressional seat Tuesday, effective 2 p.m. ET, as a new accuser, Lonna Drewes, held a press conference in Beverly Hills alleging that Swalwell drugged and raped her in a West Hollywood hotel room in 2018. Drewes, represented by attorneys Lisa Bloom and Arick Fudali, said she delayed coming forward due to Swalwell's "political power" and stated she plans to share evidence with the Los Angeles County Sheriff's Office.

Following the press conference, the Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department confirmed its Special Victims Bureau has opened an investigation into Drewes's claims, describing it as "in its preliminary stages and ongoing." Swalwell's attorney, Sara Azari, issued a statement saying the politician "categorically and unequivocally denies each and every allegation of sexual misconduct and assault."

Swalwell's resignation comes days after he suspended his gubernatorial campaign and the House Ethics Committee opened an investigation, following a San Francisco Chronicle report that he sexually assaulted a former staffer on two occasions. CNN subsequently reported similar allegations from three additional women involving unwanted explicit messages or photos.

The accumulation of allegations and the resignation leave California's 14th congressional district without representation pending a special election. The case has drawn significant attention both because of Swalwell's prior national profile — including his role on the House Intelligence Committee — and the serious criminal nature of the newest allegations.

stories.headline: Justice Department Fires Four Prosecutors Over FACE Act Cases, Releases 'Weaponization' Report
stories.summary:
The Trump Justice Department on Tuesday fired four federal prosecutors who handled cases against anti-abortion activists under the Freedom of Access to Clinic Entrances (FACE) Act, accusing the Biden administration of politically motivated and selective prosecutions. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche announced the terminations alongside the first report from the department's "Weaponization Working Group," created by former Attorney General Pam Bondi to review federal prosecutions criticized by conservatives.

The report alleges that Biden-era prosecutors "ignored and downplayed" attacks against pregnancy resource centers and houses of worship — also protected under the FACE Act — while seeking harsher sentences against anti-abortion defendants. Blanche said the department "will not tolerate a two-tiered system of justice" and pledged to "restore integrity" to the prosecutorial system. President Trump last year pardoned several anti-abortion activists who had been convicted of blockading clinic entrances.

Critics of the Trump administration, including former Attorney General Merrick Garland and former Special Counsel Jack Smith, have maintained that their prosecutorial decisions were based solely on facts, evidence, and law. Opponents argue that the firings and the Weaponization Working Group itself represent a politicization of the Justice Department, using the agency to advance Trump's personal and political agenda rather than enforce the law impartially.

The FACE Act, signed in 1994, makes it a federal crime to physically obstruct or threaten individuals seeking reproductive health services, and also protects pregnancy resource centers and religious institutions. The terminations are the latest in a series of Justice Department personnel actions targeting employees perceived as insufficiently aligned with the administration's priorities.

stories.headline: Olivia Troye, Former Trump Aide Turned Critic, Launches Virginia Congressional Bid
stories.summary:
Olivia Troye, a former homeland security and counterterrorism adviser to Vice President Mike Pence who later became a prominent critic of Donald Trump, announced Tuesday that she is running for a Virginia congressional seat. Troye launched her campaign with a video in which she cast herself as the candidate best positioned to challenge the president, drawing on her insider experience in the Trump administration.

Troye gained national attention in 2020 when she broke from the administration and publicly criticized Trump's handling of the COVID-19 pandemic, later appearing in ads opposing his re-election. Her candidacy adds a notable anti-Trump Republican voice to the Democratic-leaning congressional race landscape in Virginia.

The announcement adds to a growing number of former Republican officials and administration insiders who have sought elected office running on platforms critical of Trump, reflecting ongoing intraparty tensions within the GOP. Troye's campaign is expected to focus heavily on national security and accountability themes drawn from her government service.

stories.headline: Update: Trump Says New U.S.-Iran Talks Could Begin Within Days as UN Chief Calls Restart 'Highly Probable'
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump said Tuesday that a second round of nuclear negotiations with Iran could take place "over the next two days," telling the New York Post that Islamabad remained the likely venue. The statement came as American diplomats worked through back channels to arrange fresh talks following Washington's enactment of its naval blockade of Iranian ports, with Tehran continuing to threaten retaliatory strikes against regional targets.

United Nations Secretary-General António Guterres said it was "highly probable" that U.S.-Iran negotiations would restart, citing a meeting he held with Pakistan's deputy prime minister, Ishaq Dar. Pakistan has positioned itself as a key intermediary between Washington and Tehran, and its diplomatic outreach appears to have gained traction with both the White House and the UN leadership.

Separately, the first direct talks in decades between the Israeli and Lebanese ambassadors to the United States concluded in Washington on Tuesday. Israeli Ambassador Yechiel Leiter described the meeting as "positive," saying the two countries are "on the same side of the equation" in working to limit Hezbollah's influence in Lebanon. Lebanese Ambassador Nada Hamadeh Moawad called the session "constructive," according to the U.S. State Department.

The developments represent the most concrete diplomatic momentum since the U.S.-Iran ceasefire took effect, though significant gaps remain over Iran's nuclear stockpiles and the terms of any lasting agreement. The blockade of Iranian ports continues to test the ceasefire's durability, with oil markets and regional governments watching closely for signs of whether a formal negotiating session will materialize.

stories.headline: Appeals Court Orders End to Contempt Investigation of Trump Administration Over Deportation Flights
stories.summary:
A divided three-judge panel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit ruled Tuesday that Chief Judge James Boasberg must terminate his criminal contempt investigation of the Trump administration over deportation flights that carried Venezuelan migrants to El Salvador in March 2025. The majority opinion, written by Circuit Judge Neomi Rao, held that the Trump administration had a "clear and indisputable" right to termination of the proceedings, finding that Boasberg's original restraining order did not clearly and specifically bar the government from transferring migrants into Salvadoran custody.

The case stems from a March 15, 2025 temporary restraining order Boasberg issued barring the administration from transferring a group of Venezuelan migrants to El Salvador under an 18th-century wartime law. After the order was entered, two planeloads of migrants departed the U.S. and were subsequently imprisoned in one of El Salvador's most high-security facilities. Boasberg concluded the administration may have acted in bad faith by rushing the transfers in defiance of his order.

The Trump White House welcomed the ruling, having long portrayed Boasberg as a biased judge who overstepped his authority. President Trump had previously called for Boasberg's impeachment, and the Justice Department filed a misconduct complaint against him last year over alleged improper public comments.

Attorneys for the deported migrants announced they will ask the full circuit court to review the panel's decision. ACLU lawyer Lee Gelernt called the ruling "a blow to the rule of law," arguing the case represented a clear instance of the executive branch willfully violating a court order. The ruling is the latest development in a yearlong legal battle that has become a central flashpoint in Trump's mass deportation campaign.

stories.headline: Los Angeles Schools Strike Narrowly Averted in Last-Minute Deal
stories.summary:
A threatened strike by workers in the Los Angeles Unified School District was averted Tuesday after negotiators reached a last-minute agreement, preventing a shutdown of classes for hundreds of thousands of students in the nation's second-largest school district. The deal was struck before a Tuesday strike deadline that would have closed schools across the sprawling district.

The settlement avoids a significant disruption to students and families across Los Angeles, where a work stoppage would have affected one of the most consequential urban public school systems in the country. Details of the agreement, including wage and benefit terms, were not fully disclosed in initial reports.

The last-minute nature of the agreement reflects the heightened tensions in urban school labor negotiations, a trend seen in several major American cities in recent years. Los Angeles schools have previously experienced high-profile labor actions, making Tuesday's averted strike a closely watched outcome for education policy observers nationally.

stories.headline: Democrats Introduce Bill to Create Presidential Capacity Commission Under 25th Amendment
stories.summary:
Maryland Representative Jamie Raskin introduced legislation Tuesday backed by 50 Democratic lawmakers that would establish a permanent Commission on Presidential Capacity to Discharge the Powers and Duties of Office, creating a formal body empowered to evaluate whether a sitting president is fit to serve. The bill proposes restructuring the 25th Amendment process so that the vice president would need only a majority vote from the newly formed commission — rather than a majority of the full Cabinet — to initiate removal proceedings.

The commission would be composed of members selected by the top four congressional leaders, each appointing four retired officials such as former presidents, vice presidents, or cabinet members. Additionally, four physicians and four psychiatrists would serve on the panel to assist with medical evaluations. Raskin framed the bill as a structural reform that should have been established when the 25th Amendment was ratified in 1967, rather than as a direct attack on any individual president.

Raskin cited concerns about President Trump's recent conduct, including his public feud with Pope Leo XIV, posts on social media depicting himself as Jesus Christ, and actions during the Iran conflict, as motivating factors. "Public trust in Donald Trump's ability to meet the duties of his office has dropped to unprecedented lows," Raskin said in a press release.

Republicans are expected to oppose the measure, and the bill faces long odds in the current Congress. Critics argue the legislation is a political maneuver rather than a good-faith constitutional reform, noting that Democrats have repeatedly invoked the 25th Amendment as a political tool against Republican presidents. The bill is unlikely to advance without support from members of both parties.

stories.headline: Hegseth Skipping Ukraine Defense Meeting, Sending Deputy Instead
stories.summary:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth will again skip an international meeting focused on Ukraine, instead sending a top deputy in his place, according to Politico. The decision reflects a broader pattern of senior U.S. officials reducing their direct engagement with European allies on the Ukraine conflict as the administration's foreign policy attention remains concentrated on the Iran situation.

The repeated absence of the defense secretary from high-level Ukraine coordination meetings has drawn concern among NATO allies and European defense officials, who have noted a diminishing U.S. presence at forums where military aid and strategy are discussed. Fewer top American officials have been visiting or engaging directly with longtime European partners in recent months.

The development comes as Ukraine continues to seek sustained Western military and financial support, and as European nations have signaled frustration with what some describe as reduced American leadership on the issue. The absence is likely to fuel debate both in Washington and among allies about the current administration's commitment to the defense of Ukraine.

stories.headline: Savannah Guthrie Returns to 'Today' Show After Mother's Unresolved Kidnapping
stories.summary:
NBC News anchor Savannah Guthrie returned to her role as co-host of the 'Today' show on Monday, more than two months after her 84-year-old mother disappeared from her Arizona home in what was described as a kidnapping. The case remains unresolved.

Guthrie had stepped back from her on-air duties following the disappearance. Her return marks a significant moment for the long-running morning program, which had relied on fill-in anchors during her absence.

The circumstances surrounding her mother's disappearance have not been publicly resolved, and no arrests have been reported. NBC has not issued a detailed statement regarding the return beyond confirming Guthrie was back on air.

stories.headline: Gallup: Half of U.S. Workers Now Use AI on the Job as Workforce Disruption Grows
stories.summary:
For the first time in Gallup's tracking, half of employed American adults report using artificial intelligence in their work at least a few times a year, up from 46% the previous quarter, according to a new survey of 23,717 U.S. employees conducted in February 2026. Daily AI use has reached 13%, while 28% say they use it several times a week or more. Organizational adoption also rose, with 41% of employees saying their company has integrated AI tools, up three points from the prior quarter.

The growing presence of AI is reshaping workplace dynamics in measurable ways. Employees at AI-adopting organizations report significantly higher levels of disruption — 27% say their workplace has changed in disruptive ways to a large or very large extent over the past year, compared with 17% at non-adopting firms. Those organizations are simultaneously more likely to report both hiring expansions and workforce reductions than companies that have not adopted AI.

The pattern diverges notably at large employers. Among organizations with 10,000 or more employees that have adopted AI, 33% of workers report workforce reductions versus 30% reporting expansion — the inverse of the trend at non-adopting large firms, where hiring outpaces cuts 36% to 23%. Because large employers account for a substantial share of total employment and lead in AI adoption, their staffing decisions may disproportionately shape public perception of AI's job impact.

Concerns about job displacement are rising alongside adoption rates. Eighteen percent of all U.S. employees say it is very or somewhat likely their job will be eliminated within five years due to AI or automation; that figure climbs to 23% among workers at AI-adopting organizations. Frequent AI users generally report productivity gains, though Gallup notes that evidence of fundamental, organization-wide changes to how work is done remains limited.

stories.headline: Amazon to Acquire Satellite Company Globalstar for $11.57 Billion in Challenge to SpaceX Starlink
stories.summary:
Amazon has agreed to acquire satellite communications company Globalstar for $11.57 billion, marking a major escalation in the company's ambitions to compete directly with SpaceX's Starlink internet service. The deal signals Amazon's commitment to its Project Kuiper initiative, under which it plans to deploy roughly 3,200 satellites within the next three years and launch a broadband internet service to rival Starlink's established global network.

The acquisition would give Amazon significant existing satellite infrastructure and spectrum rights, potentially accelerating its timeline to market. SpaceX's Starlink has built a substantial lead in the low-Earth orbit satellite internet sector, serving millions of customers worldwide and playing a prominent role in conflict zones including Ukraine.

The deal represents one of the largest technology acquisitions of 2026 and underscores the intensifying competition in the commercial space and satellite connectivity industry. Analysts noted that Amazon's deep capital reserves and existing cloud and logistics infrastructure could make it a formidable long-term competitor to SpaceX, though Starlink's head start remains a significant advantage.

stories.headline: OpenAI Internal Memo Accuses Anthropic of Inflating Revenue by $8 Billion, Calls Strategy Built on 'Fear'
stories.summary:
An internal OpenAI memo authored by Chief Revenue Officer Denise Dresser and published by The Verge accuses rival AI company Anthropic of overstating its revenue run rate by approximately $8 billion. Dresser alleged that Anthropic uses accounting treatment that grosses up revenue-sharing arrangements with Amazon and Google, inflating its recently disclosed $30 billion annual run rate figure. OpenAI, she wrote, reports its Microsoft revenue-share arrangement on a net basis, which she described as more in line with standard accounting practice.

The memo, directed at OpenAI's sales organization, also criticized Anthropic's broader competitive strategy, characterizing it as "built on fear, restriction, and the idea that a small group of elites should control AI." Dresser further argued that Anthropic made a strategic error by not securing sufficient computing capacity and that its heavy focus on coding products leaves it vulnerable as AI adoption spreads across industries beyond software development.

Anthropic has not publicly responded to the memo's allegations. The company disclosed the $30 billion run rate figure last week, representing roughly a tripling of its growth since the end of 2025. The public surfacing of the internal memo highlights the intensifying commercial rivalry between the two leading American AI laboratories as enterprise adoption accelerates.

stories.headline: Nvidia Denies Acquisition Talks with Dell After Rumor Sends PC Stocks Surging
stories.summary:
Shares of Dell Technologies jumped nearly 7% and HP climbed more than 5% on Monday following a market rumor that Nvidia was in discussions to acquire a personal computer manufacturer. The gains reversed in after-hours trading after an Nvidia spokesperson flatly denied the report, stating, "The media report is false; Nvidia is not engaged in discussions to acquire any PC maker."

The episode highlights the volatile sentiment surrounding artificial intelligence infrastructure stocks, which have been whipsawed in recent weeks by both the ongoing Iran conflict and broader uncertainty about Big Tech valuations. Investors have struggled to separate war-related risk aversion from underlying shifts in sentiment toward major technology companies.

Separately, Meta is reportedly developing a photorealistic AI avatar of CEO Mark Zuckerberg, according to the Financial Times. The avatar is being trained to adopt Zuckerberg's mannerisms and tone, with the stated goal of giving employee feedback and helping staff better understand company strategy. Meta has also been building a separate "CEO agent" tool designed to rapidly retrieve information to assist Zuckerberg directly.

Meanwhile, anticipation continues to build in markets around two high-profile potential public offerings: a possible IPO from AI company Anthropic as early as this year, and the long-awaited public debut of Elon Musk's SpaceX.

stories.headline: Update: Pakistan Proposes New U.S.-Iran Talks as Blockade Tests Begin and Red Cross Delivers First Aid to Iran
stories.summary:
Pakistan has proposed a new round of U.S.-Iran negotiations as the naval blockade of Iranian ports entered its first days of active enforcement. A chemical and oil tanker, the Rich Starry, aborted an attempt to exit the Strait of Hormuz on Monday before successfully transiting the waterway early Tuesday, marking one of the first direct tests of the U.S. blockade's reach. The U.S. military has clarified that the blockade applies only to vessels traveling to or from Iranian ports, representing an apparent scaling back of President Trump's earlier threat to fully close the strait.

The International Committee of the Red Cross delivered its first emergency aid shipment to Iran since the conflict began more than a month ago, dispatching five truckloads of blankets, hygiene kits, solar lamps, and other supplies to the Iranian Red Crescent Society. Nine additional aid trucks are expected to be delivered later this week, with the total shipment projected to meet the needs of nearly 25,000 people. The delivery marks a significant humanitarian milestone as international pressure mounts over civilian conditions inside Iran.

French President Emmanuel Macron and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced they will co-chair a conference in Paris on Friday, bringing together non-belligerent nations to discuss a defensive mission to restore freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz. European and other partners have signaled readiness to contribute to tanker escort operations when security conditions allow. The initiative reflects growing international concern over the blockade's impact on global energy markets and trade.

Markets responded positively to signals that Iran had reached out to the Trump administration expressing openness to a deal. Asia's main stock indexes surged Tuesday, with Japan's Nikkei 225 rising as much as 2.5 percent and South Korea's KOSPI gaining about 3.7 percent. Brent crude dipped below $98 a barrel, retreating from highs above $103 reached earlier in the week, though analysts warned the underlying energy disruption remains severe with Strait of Hormuz transits still running at roughly one-sixth of pre-war levels.

stories.headline: Update: Pope Leo XIV Begins Historic Africa Tour in Algeria, Renews Peace Call Amid Trump Feud
stories.summary:
Pope Leo XIV arrived in Algiers on Monday, beginning the first-ever papal visit to Algeria and an 11-day apostolic tour of four African nations — Algeria, Cameroon, Angola, and Equatorial Guinea. The trip marks a historic milestone for the Catholic Church and brings the first U.S.-born pope into the rapidly growing heart of global Catholicism. Leo held a special Mass inside the Basilica of Our Lady of Africa and visited the Great Mosque in Algiers, where he promoted Christian-Muslim coexistence and honored St. Augustine, the locally born theologian central to Leo's religious spirituality.

In his remarks in Algiers, Leo called for peace and an end to what he described as 'neocolonial tendencies' in world affairs, framing his message explicitly against the backdrop of the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict. The visit began under the shadow of a sharpening public feud with President Trump, who said overnight that Leo was 'not doing a very good job' as pope, called him 'a very liberal person,' and suggested he should 'stop catering to the Radical Left.' Leo responded by saying his appeals for peace and reconciliation are rooted in the Gospel and that he did not fear the Trump administration.

The exchange marks a significant escalation in the public rift between the White House and the Vatican, which has previously been covered in this publication. Monday's new developments — Leo's first in-country remarks from Algiers and Trump's overnight public broadside — represent concrete new statements not previously reported. The pope's Africa itinerary is expected to draw further global attention as he addresses conflict, migration, and interfaith relations across the continent over the coming 11 days.

stories.headline: Update: Asian Markets Surge, Oil Falls Below $98 as Iran Signals Openness to Deal
stories.summary:
Asian equity markets rallied sharply on Tuesday after President Trump said Iranian officials had contacted his administration and expressed a desire to reach a deal. Japan's Nikkei 225 rose as much as 2.5 percent, South Korea's KOSPI gained about 3.7 percent, and the rally followed a 1 percent gain in the S&P 500 on Wall Street. The moves represent a meaningful reversal from the steep market declines that followed the announcement of the Hormuz blockade earlier in the week.

Brent crude fell nearly 1.5 percent, dropping below $98 a barrel after having surged above $103 when Trump first threatened to blockade the Strait of Hormuz. The U.S. military's subsequent clarification that the blockade would apply only to vessels entering and exiting Iranian ports — rather than a full closure of the strait — helped ease some of the worst-case energy market fears. Still, analysts cautioned that the underlying supply disruption remains severe, with strait transits running at roughly one-sixth of pre-war daily levels.

The market movements illustrate how closely global investors are tracking diplomatic signals in the Iran conflict, with even tentative indications of talks producing significant price swings. The IMF and World Bank had previously signaled plans to downgrade global growth forecasts in response to the war's economic fallout. Whether the renewed diplomatic contact translates into formal negotiations remains uncertain, with no confirmed talks scheduled as of Tuesday morning.
